resume:
JORDAN KELLER
Junior Software Engineer | Seattle, WA

OBJECTIVE
Recent CS grad seeking a backend engineering role. About 2 years of professional experience.

EXPERIENCE
Software Engineer I, Tessellate Software (Jan 2023 – present)
- Backend developer on the payments team, Node.js and PostgreSQL.
- Led the redesign of the refunds service. (Note: in the bullet below I describe myself as having 'assisted' on this same refunds work — I led the API design but assisted on the data-migration part.)
- Owned on-call rotation for the team.

Software Engineering Intern, Tessellate Software (Summer 2022)
- Converted to full-time after the internship.
- Worked on internal tooling in Python.

Freelance Web Developer (2021 – 2022)
- Built small business websites. (Header says ~2 years professional experience; counting freelance plus full-time it's closer to 3, but I usually only count the full-time year and a half.)

EDUCATION
B.S. Computer Science, University of Washington — graduated 2022. (Internship was Summer 2022; I list the FT start as Jan 2023, so there is a gap I usually explain as finishing coursework in fall.)

SKILLS
JavaScript/TypeScript, Node.js, Express, PostgreSQL, Python, Git, Docker (basic), some AWS.
jd: Backend Engineer (Early Career). Join our payments platform team building services that process refunds, disputes, and settlement. You'll write Node.js/TypeScript services backed by PostgreSQL, participate in on-call, and own well-scoped features end to end. We're looking for someone early in their career who can clearly explain a piece of code they wrote — what problem it solved, the approach, and how they verified it worked. Strong fundamentals and clear communication matter more than years of experience.

history.q: Tell me a bit about your role on the payments team — what do you work on day to day?
You mention redesigning the refunds service. What problem was the redesign solving?
What part of that did you personally lead versus contribute to?
Walk me through the refund API you designed — the key endpoint and its inputs/outputs.
How did you handle a refund that partially fails — say the payment processor times out mid-request?
Concretely, how did you make that retry safe so a customer isn't refunded twice?

candidate_last_answer: Right, yeah, so — okay so the double-refund thing, that's, um, that's a really good question. So basically we, um, we didn't want to refund someone twice, obviously, that would be really bad, so we had like a — sorry, let me think. So there was an idempotency thing, I think we used an idempotency key? Or — actually it might have been that we just checked the database first to see if a refund row existed, I'm honestly blanking a little. We definitely talked about idempotency keys in design review, I remember that, but I'm not a hundred percent sure if that's what shipped or if my teammate did that part. Sorry, I get a bit nervous on this stuff. Um, I think it worked though, we didn't get double-refund tickets, so.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I'm not a hundred percent sure if that's what shipped or if my teammate did that part

expected-yield: a definite answer on which mechanism actually shipped — idempotency key versus a pre-insert existence check — and whether the candidate personally implemented it, pinning the exact column/key and uniqueness constraint used
